focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The substrate went quiet around dawn.

Gavin noticed it the way you notice a headache ending — not the sudden absence of pressure but the slow realization that it had been there and now it wasn't. For hours the battle's output had saturated everything: hundreds of spells fired simultaneously, each one flexing the agreements that made it possible, the accumulated noise of it drowning out the fine structures he needed to perceive the way a brass band drowns out a whisper. He'd sat with the manuscript open on his knees and stared at notation he couldn't read because every time his perception tried to settle, another volley of combat magic dragged the substrate sideways and the patterns dissolved.

He didn't know how long the battle had lasted. Time had a different quality in the sub-collection even on normal nights — the old stone didn't carry sound from the castle above, and the self-lit walls held the same faint glow whether it was noon or midnight. But the substrate carried everything, and through it Gavin had felt the fighting as a continuous distortion, the magical equivalent of trying to read during an earthquake. He'd tried to work anyway. He'd sat on the limestone floor with his back against the shelf where he'd first found the manuscript seven months ago, and he'd held the pages open, and he'd stared at notation that blurred and re-formed every time a cluster of killing curses hit the same stretch of corridor two hundred feet above his head. He'd accomplished nothing.

And then it stopped. There was a trailing-off, a scattering of final percussive jolts — single spells, then silence, then another, then silence that held — and then a quiet that spread outward through the substrate until the last resonance died out. The agreements were still there, sti...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...rint in the outer corridor kept dragging at the edge of his attention. He brushed grit off the lower half of the page and narrowed his focus. Four parameters. Five. A brief flash of six before the substrate vibrated and he lost the fifth.

More feet passed at irregular intervals. Once he heard someone say, "Hold that there," followed by the wet cough of somebody trying not to choke. Once the old stone overhead rang hard enough to sting his teeth. The weight of the wizarding world's unresolved conflict was concentrating on this castle, tonight, and the building hummed with it.

The notation trembled. He lost his fifth parameter again.

He sat in the dry limestone cold of the sub-collection, the manuscript open before him, the quartz weights ticking whenever the floor answered the noise above. The wards overhead were thin and thinning. The thing beyond them continued in its way, which was not malicious, which was not intentional, which was simply incompatible with everything that existed below the wards in the way too much load was incompatible with a beam already splitting along the grain — not through will but through the dull fact of pressure. His usual route out was gone. Smoke and blood smell had reached even here. The notation jittered on the page.

Gavin Tench, fifteen years old, mediocre at everything except the one thing that mattered and not good enough at that, sat on the stone floor with the blocked corridor behind him and tried to hold six variables in his mind while the world above him prepared to tear itself apart over a problem he could solve in seconds and wouldn't, because the wards were failing, and the wards were all that mattered, and the wards did not care about the war.

He brushed dust from the margin and turned a page. The notation moved. He read.
